Understanding the Machine

The device executing your program is built around a CPU—a collection of independent execution engines called cores.

A core is a hardware unit capable of executing a sequence of instructions. It contains its own execution pipeline, registers, and control logic. Each core operates independently.

Modern processors contain multiple such cores. This is referred to as a multicore CPU. If a processor has (N) cores, it can execute up to (N) independent instruction streams in parallel.

A thread is not hardware. It is a software-defined execution context consisting of:

  • a program counter
  • a register state
  • a stack

Threads are scheduled by the operating system onto cores. A core executes instructions from one thread at a time, unless it supports Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT).

In processors with SMT, a single core can maintain multiple thread contexts and issue instructions from them in an interleaved manner. This improves utilization of the core’s pipeline but does not double its computational capacity.

Parallel Execution

Parallelism arises from multiple cores.

If there are:

  • (C) cores
  • (T) threads

Then:

  • If (T <= C): threads can execute truly in parallel
  • If (T > C): threads are time-sliced

SMT allows multiple threads to share a single core, but this is a latency-hiding mechanism, not a source of linear speedup.

Memory Hierarchy

The processor does not operate directly on data from main memory in most cases. Instead, it relies on a hierarchy:

  • Registers (inside the core, fastest)
  • L1 / L2 / L3 caches (on-chip, small but fast)
  • Main memory (RAM)
  • Secondary storage (SSD/HDD)

Data movement becomes progressively slower and more expensive as we go down this hierarchy.

For performance analysis, the critical boundary is between:

  • on-chip computation
  • off-chip memory (RAM)

Compute vs Data Movement

Any program performs two fundamental actions:

  1. Compute — arithmetic operations executed by the core
  2. Data movement — transferring data between memory and the core

Performance is limited by whichever of these becomes the bottleneck.

To quantify this, we define:

  • Peak compute throughput: maximum operations per second a CPU can perform
  • Memory bandwidth: maximum rate at which data can be transferred from memory

Arithmetic Intensity

Arithmetic intensity connects compute and memory:

$ \text{Arithmetic Intensity} = \frac{\text{Total Floating Point Operations}}{\text{Total Bytes Moved}} $

  • Low intensity → memory-bound
  • High intensity → compute-bound

Toward the Roofline Model

The roofline model was first introduced by a group of scientists at the University of California, Berkely in 2008, in a paper titled:

Roofline: An Insightful Visual Performance Model for Floating-Point Programs 
and Multicore Architectures

By Samuel Williams, Andrew Waterman and David Patterson

The Roofline model combines:

  • peak compute throughput
  • memory bandwidth
  • operational intensity

to determine the maximum achievable performance of a program on a given machine.

It provides a quantitative way to answer:

  • Is the program limited by compute or memory?
  • How far is it from hardware limits?

The following formula drives the two performance limits in the graph:
$ \text{Attainable GFlops/sec =} $ $ \text{Min(Peak Floating Point Performance, Peak Memory Bandwidth * Operational Intensity)} $

Building a Roofline Model

The following parameters are required to construct a roofline model. More rigorous the calculations, more closer to truth is the roofline model.

  • Peak Sustained Memory Bandwidth: Ideally from a memory bandwidth benchmarking suit - eg. Triad from STREAM, published by Dr. Jphn D McCalpin, University of Virginia) (check out to see how Glacier.HPC used the TRIAD kernel to observe the peak sustained memory bandwidth) $ \text{Bandwidth = \frac{\text{Bytes transferred}}{\text{Execution time}}} $

  • Peak Compute Throughput: Calculated using -
    $ \text{Peak compute} = \text{No. of cores * sustained frequency * FLOPs per instruction cycle} $

  • Arithmatic Intensity: Calculated using - $ \text{FLOPs / Bytes moved} $

  • Arithmatic Intensity Ridge Point: Calculated using - $ \text{Arithmatic Intensity Ridge Point} = \frac{\text{Peak compute throughput}}{\text{Memory bandwidth}} \text{FLOPS/byte} $

  • The Roofline Bound Calculated using the principal formula, also cited above -
    $ \text{Attainable GFlops/sec =} $ $ \text{Min(Peak Floating Point Performance, Peak Memory Bandwidth * Operational Intensity)} $

Viasualizing a Roofline Model

The original paper (cited above) demonstrates the roofline model on four different chipsets of it’s time. Here is the roofline model characterising AMD’s Ryzen 5 6600H processor.

X-axis: Arithmatic Intensity on the log scale. Y-axis: Performance (FLOPs/sec) on the log scale.

The Roofline model is conceptually differentiated into two regimes, which all the workload inevitably fall into:

  • Memory bound: The performance is bound and scaled with the memory bandwidth.
  • Compute bound: The performance is capped by the peak compute capacity of the physical cores.

The green curve comprising of two straight lines represents the peak achievable performance pertaining to the workload’s Arithmatic Intensity (hereby referred to as AI).
The diagonally placed line in green represents the Memory bound regime, whereas the horizontally placed line in green represents the Compute bound regime.

Using the Roofline Model

The roofline model is a diagnostic of the workload’s nature. It merely places the workload on a coordinate system, which otherwise feels very abstract.

The goal remains to increase the workload’s AI, to push it as much to the right of the graph as possible. This ensures that the cores are increasingly saturated than before, thereby extracting more porformance from the existing hardware.

As the AI increases, the workload moves diagonally up along the green line, until it eventually and hopefully plateaus along the vertical line in green. Once it plateaus, performance is extracted by increasing the hardware’s compute efficiency.

Glacier.HPC

Formally titled as

Glacier.HPC Profiling, Benchmarking and Analysis of Numerical Kernels derived from common Supervised Machine Learning 
Algorithms on consumer grade computing hardware.

the project contains several iterative optimization experiments on numerical kernels, with their roofline plots and related discussions.

Glacier.HPC is derived from the author’s previous project where ML numerical algoriths were implemented from scratch in C++. It focuses on:

  • measuting arithmatic intensity, performance measures
  • constructing roofline models
  • evaluating optimization stratergies to finally integrate the results into Glacier.ML.

This is the author’s undergraduate degree major project.
